data.title: National HealthCare: 50% Return In The Past 6 Months And Still Undervalued
data.description:
National HealthCare (NHC) has had a nice run in the recent months. Total return is 17% in the past 3 months and 51% in the past 6 months:

The gains appear to have been driven by earnings growth, with valuation ratios nearly unchanged from year-ago levels. EV/EBITDA is currently in the low double digits, which could indicate that the market is not expecting high earnings growth in the next few years.

However, I continue to believe that high growth is the more likely outcome, and NHC remains the largest position in my portfolio. Inpatient stays in rehab hospitals and traditional hospitals are incredibly expensive. Recent trends of moving some of that inpatient burden to far less expensive skilled nursing facilities will continue. The result of this will be a continued increase in nursing home occupancy and patient acuity.

For those that may not be familiar with the company, NHC owns and operates skilled nursing facilities, assisted living facilities, and independent living facilities. NHC also owns and operates home health, home personal care, and hospice agencies. Skilled nursing facilities are really the heart of the business, contributing approximately 85% of total revenue. Although NHC owns the majority of facilities that it operates, it rents a significant portion from National Health Investors (NHI). The master lease expires at the end of 2026, and lease renewal negotiations are ongoing.

This article includes:

  • A review of NHC's strong 3rd quarter results and a 4th quarter earnings preview.
  • A section about the lease renewal between NHC and NHI, with a potential solution that would benefit both companies.
  • Valuation using fair value models
  • Potential hidden value in NHC's owned properties
  • Overview and commentary on the primary risks the company faces

3rd Quarter Results and 4th Quarter Preview

In November, NHC once again reported strong quarterly earnings. Highlights included adjusted diluted EPS up 24.4% and total revenue up 12.5% compared to the same quarter in 2024.

The company's fortress balance sheet strengthened even more. Cash increased by $19.6M, and $36.9M of debt was paid off. At the end of the 3rd quarter, NHC had $64M more cash than debt.

Income from operations in the 3rd quarter was up 44% year on year but appeared to decline sequentially from the 2nd quarter 2025:

However, an earnings release footnote stated that a $4.48M quality incentive Medicare Advantage payment was delayed. Had the payment been received before the reporting deadline, 3rd quarter income from operations would have been $34.82M.

The current incentive payment and the delayed payment are both highly likely to have already been received in the 4th quarter. NHC is not waiting on payment from CMS or an external entity. NHC Advantage managed care, owned by NHC, had the money, and there was a timing delay.

A double quality incentive payment will boost 4th quarter income from operations. To project this value, the 3rd quarter value plus the missing payment can be used as a starting point: $34.82M. Then, the current period incentive payment of $4.48M is added, giving a projected 4th quarter operating income of $39.3M. This value added to the actual values for the other 3 quarters gives a projected total 2025 income from operations: $134.5M:

The table below gives EBITDA and GAAP earnings for the past few quarters:

EBITDA is a useful metric that correlates strongly with income from operations. GAAP earnings per share is a much less useful metric because standardized accounting requires the inclusion of real estate depreciation and unrealized gains on equity holdings.

EBITDA is projected to be strong in the 4th quarter, again due to the probable double quality incentive payment received. This would bring total EBITDA for 2025 to $178.8M. Total 2024 EBITDA was $122.4M. Projected annual EBITDA growth: 46.1%.

Moving on to GAAP EPS, the projected 2025 value is $8.03. The actual value in 2024 was: $6.53. Projected GAAP EPS growth: 23.0%.

You may notice in the table above that 4th quarter 2024 EPS appears to have been very weak. This was primarily due to a major drop in NHI's stock price, from $84.06 to $69.30. Standardized accounting required NHC to record a major loss in the "unrealized gains/loss on marketable equity securities" section. After the next earnings are released, NHC's projected strong 2025 4th quarter results will replace the weak 2024 4th quarter results. This will lead to a sharp drop in trailing P/E. This metric is currently 22.1, but after the results are released, it will be around 17.8 at the current stock price.

Lease Renewal Situation and a Potential Solution

NHC owns the majority of facilities that they operate. However, a significant portion of NHC facilities are leased from NHI:

My last coverage detailed significant events in the lease renewal process that occurred in the 3rd quarter of 2025. Since September, there have been no new public actions or filings by either company related to lease renewal. The absence of filings is a positive for both companies. In the 3rd quarter, relations between NHC and NHI appeared to be turning increasingly hostile. And hostility between business partners is not what you want to see as a shareholder of either company. For now, the situation appears to have calmed.

I continue to believe NHI will not attempt to change operators for this portfolio because this scenario could be an absolute nightmare for NHI shareholders. Many of the facilities in the portfolio are located in small, isolated communities that cover a vast geographic area, for example, Moulton, AL; Desloge, MO; and Glasgow, KY. Finding a new operator that could competently run facilities in all of these locations would be extremely difficult.

NHI has two viable options:

  1. They can renew the rent at a true fair market rate.

  2. NHI can sell all or part of the portfolio to NHC at true fair market value.

I now think the best solution for both companies is for NHC to purchase the entire portfolio. To show that a full portfolio purchase is possible and beneficial for both companies, first an estimate of fair market sale price is needed.

This is an estimate, and true market value could be higher or lower. The goal is to give a general idea of a potential sales price and to demonstrate the feasibility of a full portfolio transaction.

The most recently available industry reports for skilled nursing facilities indicate an average sale price per bed of $83,800. However, this value includes many distressed sales of failing facilities. Market price per bed varies greatly depending on occupancy levels, age of the buildings, geographic location, and acuity (higher acuity, short-term stay facilities sell at a premium).

Recent portfolio sales are more useful to estimate the market value of the NHI portfolio leased to NHC:

  1. NHC itself purchased the White Oak skilled nursing facility portfolio in 2024. NHC paid approximately $90,000 for each skilled nursing bed. These were not distressed properties, but they also weren't high-acuity, high-turnover properties.
  2. In 2025, a 5000-bed skilled nursing facility portfolio that covered seven states sold for $545M, or $109,000 per bed, to an anonymous buyer.
  3. CareTrust REIT purchased a skilled nursing facility portfolio in 2024. They paid $151,000 for each skilled nursing bed. These properties were high-acuity, high-turnover, rehab-focused facilities.

The portfolio NHC rents from NHI is a mix of facilities: some are focused on traditional long-term care and others are more focused on high-acuity post-hospital stays. In my last coverage of the company, I found an average acuity ratio in the portfolio to be at 0.99, just slightly below the national average of 1.0. The buildings are older, generally having been built decades ago. This portfolio is not at all comparable to the high-acuity-focused CareTrust portfolio (#3 above). However, because NHC is a quality operator and the properties have good profitability now, some premium valuation over the White Oak portfolio and the anonymous buyer portfolio (#1 and 2 above) is justified. $117,500 per bed will be used to estimate the market value of the skilled nursing facilities in the NHI portfolio.

Assisted and independent living facilities are a very small portion of the portfolio. $150,000 per unit will be used for these facilities. This is towards the lower end of average national sales price ranges because they are older properties.

An estimate for the total purchase price is $488.9M:

In the most recent quarterly report, NHC had $130.6M cash and $65.6M debt. NHC needs approximately $490M to purchase the entire portfolio by Dec. 31st, 2026, when the lease expires. Unlevered free cash flow (from the valuation section below), is $110M for 2025, and $115M for 2026. NHC pays out $9.9M in dividends each quarter. The remainder of the cash flow after the dividend payment can be saved for the portfolio purchase. That amount is $17.6M per quarter in 2025 and $18.9M per quarter in 2026. Another $15M will be available due to net interest from the cash and dividends from marketable equity securities. At year-end 2026, they should have around $174M in cash and zero long-term debt.

Because of required cash reserves, they could use around $100M of that $174M for the purchase of the portfolio. A $390M loan would be required for the remainder. NHC would then have a total debt/EBITDA ratio of around 2.1x and a total debt/total shareholder equity ratio of around 0.39. The main point here is that NHC is able to purchase the entire NHI portfolio at the end of 2026, and they would still not be anywhere close to being overleveraged.

NHC is managed conservatively. For years, the company had near-zero long-term debt. For the first time in at least a decade, NHC took out a significant loan to buy the White Oak Portfolio in 2024. Fifteen months later, the majority of that loan was already paid back.

The loan required to buy the NHI properties would be three times the size of the White Oak loan. Management may be reluctant to take on a loan of this size. However, I believe it's a productive use of capital for a number of reasons:

  • NHC would not become over leveraged

  • The NHI portfolio is already highly profitable. They aren't taking over the operations of new facilities. There's no uncertainty about profitability here.

  • There are no operator turnover costs. No new equipment purchases are required. No staff turnover related to a management change.

  • Interest payments would be lower than current rent. Rent is around $40M, and interest would be around $22M the first year, before declining sharply if the loan was paid off completely in 5 years.

  • No increase in capex. The company is currently responsible for capex under the terms of the lease with NHI

A full portfolio sale also benefits NHI. Unlike NHC, NHI is structured as a REIT. They own skilled nursing facilities and senior housing. Traditionally, these types of REITs received a fixed rent payment from operators (like NHC). The fixed payment is still generally used for skilled nursing leases. However, these types of REITs now prefer senior housing leases that set rent as a percentage of operator profits.

This profit sharing has been incredibly successful for these REIT types. Welltower (WELL) has used these percentage based leases at the largest scale. Its stock price is up well over 100% in the past two years. REITs in this subsector are now investing exclusively in senior housing and even selling other property types to fund these purchases. LTC, for example, recently sold skilled nursing properties to fund senior housing purchases.

NHI is also rapidly expanding their senior housing operating portfolio. A huge cash influx from a sale of the portfolio leased to NHC could almost immediately propel NHI into becoming a leader in senior housing. NHI's older, fixed-rent properties often have fixed-rate escalators around 2% to keep up with inflation. Instead, they could invest in properties with 10% growth for continuing operations, in addition to the initial yield.

Valuation

Two different models are presented in this section. In the first model, NHC renews the lease, and rent is increased. In the second, NHC gets a bank loan and purchases the properties at the end of 2026.

Revenue growth of 4% and a time period before terminal growth of five years were used in both models. Past revenue growth for continuing operations alone has been nearly 10% in recent years, while occupancy and acuity continue to trend up. And the growth runway before terminal growth for skilled nursing is likely much longer than the 5 years used in the models, because of demographics. The inputs (revenue growth and time before terminal growth) were chosen to make the projection conservative and to provide a margin of safety.

First, the rent model:

This model assumes zero new property acquisitions, a significant rent increase after 2026, and 4% revenue growth (conservative). Even after those inputs, the fair value estimate is $153, 7% higher than the current stock price.

The buy model:

This model assumes NHC purchases the entire NHI portfolio at the end of 2026 for $490M. Rent drops to nearly zero after the purchase. Revenue growth is again set at a conservative 4% growth. No additional acquisitions are modeled. First, the model demonstrates NHC would have significant upside to get to a fair value of $173. Second, it demonstrates that a purchase of the portfolio would increase the value of the company by quite a large amount, compared to continued rent ($173 vs. $153).

Depreciation and Hidden Value

Including all 3 facility types, NHC currently owns 7208 beds/units. Again, standard accounting requires NHC to depreciate their properties while market value likely increases. The book value of all NHC-owned properties and equipment was $675.2M at the end of the 3rd quarter. To estimate the market value of the properties, the same per-bed/unit values used in the NHI portfolio estimate will be used:

An estimate for the market value of NHC's owned properties is $902.2M. This value is $227M, or 35%, higher than book value (which also includes equipment). This gap between market and book value will likely balloon over the next few years for 2 reasons:

  1. The White Oak acquisition is still relatively recent (2nd half of 2024). It increased NHC's owned bed count by 46% and also increased the required amount of depreciation the company must record each quarter.

  2. Even if NHC does not purchase properties from the NHI portfolio, other acquisitions are likely due to the success of the White Oak purchase and the strength of NHC's balance sheet.

The rate of depreciation recorded each quarter has already increased and will likely increase further. In a few years, the gap between market and book value could be many hundreds of millions of dollars.

The Ensign Group (ENSG), a peer skilled nursing operator, faced a similar situation in 2021. Management believed the market was not fully appreciating the value of the properties they owned. Their solution was to create a captive REIT, completely owned and managed by Ensign. They moved most of their owned properties to this captive REIT. Those properties were then required to be assessed by third-party appraisers using GAAP accounting, and the results were submitted to the SEC. The market value was found to be $280M, or 40%, higher than book value.

The announcement of the appraised value was made during an earnings release after market close on February 9th, 2022. ENSG stock was up 5.3% after the announcement despite a rare revenue miss and the S&P 500 falling by 1.8% on the same day. The stock continued to rise for the remainder of February 2022, while the broader market was down 5% during the same time period.

If NHC is able to get their properties appraised under GAAP accounting standards (in 2 to 4 years, for example), the potential impact could be huge.

Primary Risks

NHI Could Attempt to Change Operators

Reasons why this scenario is improbable were given above in the "Lease Renewal" section. To further illustrate these arguments, here is an example of a distressed skilled nursing facility sale. These facilities sold for $36K per bed compared to the $117K per bed estimate for the NHI portfolio operated by NHC. An operator change would bring a huge amount of risk for NHI.

Cuts or Stagnation of Reimbursement from Govt. Insurance

I believe this outcome is unlikely because skilled nursing is the most cost-effective option for post-acute inpatient stays. In the past, the higher cost of inpatient rehabilitation facilities was justified because patient discharges were believed to be much less likely to be readmitted to acute care hospitals.

However, a recent study found that these differences were likely due to patient selection, with rehab hospitals having a younger and generally healthier patient base. The study found no significant difference in readmission rates between the 2 facility types when sample groups were adjusted for age and comorbidities.

If government insurance cuts skilled nursing reimbursement significantly, that leads to staff cuts and facility closures. That means more short-term, post-acute stays in expensive rehab hospitals or traditional hospitals. Cuts to skilled nursing actually end up raising costs for the government, and are counterproductive in the long term. In March 2025, medpac.gov recommended reimbursement rate increases in 2026 for skilled nursing facilities and cuts for inpatient rehab hospitals. There is a strong possibility this pattern of rate changes continues in the next few years.

Conclusion

NHC never holds earnings calls, does not provide any forward guidance, and until recently had a market cap under $2B. These factors can make the company less appealing and accessible for potential investors. However, when the company's financial reports are examined closely, it becomes apparent that this is a very healthy company with a lot of future potential.

data.title: Lowe's: Pace Of Recovery May Disappoint
data.description:
Shares of Lowe's (LOW) have been on a roller coaster ride over the past year, but shares are now near 52-week highs and up 6% from last year as hopes build for higher housing-related spending in 2026. I last covered Lowe's in September, when I downgraded the stock to a "Sell." That call initially played out with shares falling by 14% and hitting my $220 price target, though they have since recovered and are now 7% higher from my September coverage. With updated financials and optimism about the 2026 housing market outlook, now is a good time to revisit Lowe's.

Macro Rebound Will Be Slow

Lowe's has battled through a period of weak housing-related consumer spending, but hopes are building that there could be a rebound. I view the outlook as more nuanced. Speaking at a conference in December, management noted that while it viewed economic fundamentals as sound, its customers' sentiment has been a "little repressed," which continues to limit discretionary DIY activity and big-ticket purchases. Now, I do not expect the level of caution to get worse, so this will not cause a decline in sales going forward. Rather, it is likely to continue to cap the pace of growth, as I do not see consumer caution disappearing over the next 6-18 months.

Lowe's is particularly exposed to renovation, repair, and remodel work. In theory, the low level of existing home sales should be a positive, as homeowners with low rates stay in place and, instead of moving, remodel their current home. However, this spending has been muted as consumers remain budget-conscious and avoid discretionary projects. Homeowners often fund these projects with home equity loans, and with the strong appreciation in home prices over the past 6 years, there is substantial equity that could be tapped. However, just because there is capacity to borrow does not mean that borrowing will occur. As you can see below, while HELOC balances have grown, the pace has been modest, and balances are still below pre-COVID levels.

The rise in interest rates, combined with affordability challenges, has greatly dampened demand for HELOCs. With rates now having dropped from their peak, we may see some growth in HELOC activity and home renovation work, but I am cautious on the magnitude of the rebound in 2026, especially if home prices nationally are soft, as I expect (in general, people are more willing to invest in their house when they view it as an appreciating asset). Beyond that, credit standards for HELOCs remain tight. As you can see, the share of banks tightening standards on these loans still exceeds the share that is easing standards. While the level of tightening is not as bad as during the 2023 period as housing fundamentals turned, standards are not loose. We will likely need to see more easing from banks to truly unlock a HELOC-financed renovation cycle. All else equal, DIY work should be better for Lowe's in 2026 than 2025, but I am cautious on the pace of recovery.

Given its growing pro-business dynamics, the new construction market will start to matter more, though this remains a clear minority of its overall exposure. As you can see below, home construction has been in a multiyear recession as elevated rates and challenged affordability weighed. With a subdued pace of housing starts, I expect this to continue to decline for at least another 6-9 months before bottoming and recovering in 2027. The end of declines is in sight, but I do not believe we are there just yet.

Now, Lowe's has done a reasonable job of navigating through this weak macro environment. In the company's most recent financials reported back in November, Lowe's earned $3.06 per share, which beat estimates by $0.11 as revenue rose 3% to $20.8 billion. Earnings rose 6% from last year. Gross margins expanded 50 bps to 34.2%, a solid outcome given some tariff headwinds, though this was partially offset by SG&A inflation. As a result, operating margins expanded a more modest 10 bps to 12.4%. Same-store sales were up 0.4%. The average ticket price was up 3.4%, while transactions fell by 3%. Inventory is down over 2% from last year, which is helping to support gross margins.

Buybacks Are on Pause Post-M&A

We have also seen the company shift capital allocation strategies to focus on M&A. In October, it completed its acquisition of Foundation Building Materials ("FBM"), part of an ongoing strategy to use M&A to bolster its product offerings in the pro segment, an area where the retailer has historically lagged behind Home Depot (HD). This was an $8.8 billion deal that gives Lowe's a stronger footprint in interior building products like drywall, insulation, and plaster. It should add about $6.5 billion in sales, though it will dilute Lowe's margins, given its more commodity-like product profile. This deal followed the $1.3 billion purchase of Artisan Design Group (ADG), which was included in Q3 results.

Over the first nine months of 2025, Lowe's did just $29 million of buybacks, down from $2.26 billion in the first nine months of 2024. Because of M&A, Lowe's balance sheet has weakened, and it has nearly $40 billion of debt. As a result, leverage now stands at 3.4x, which is above its 2.75x target. Lowe's aims to achieve this again by mid-2027. On its last conference call, management downplayed the prospect for significant M&A or buybacks until this is reached. Instead, it needs to focus on restoring financial targets and also successfully integrating these acquisitions before considering M&A. In the meantime, investors will collect a 1.7% dividend yield. Longer term, I expect additional M&A and less of a singular focus on share buybacks that had been Lowe's norm from 2015 to 2024.

Shares Are Very Expensive, Making LOW a 'Sell'

Alongside results, Lowe's tightened fiscal year guidance to about $12.25 in EPS from $12.20-12.45 previously as operating margins are expected to be 12.1% from 12.2-12.3%. This drop primarily reflects a quarter's contribution from FBM and its sub-10% margin. EPS guidance is at the low end of the $12.20-12.40 that I was forecasting. I expect fiscal year 2025 results to end up in the $12.22-12.32 range.

Similar to when I last covered shares, Lowe's scores very poorly on Seeking Alpha's valuation screener, receiving a "D-." As you can see below, Lowe's is trading at a meaningful premium to both the sector and its own historic valuation. To some extent, this reflects the view that earnings are somewhat depressed vs. long-run potential given a multiyear housing slump, leading to a cyclically higher multiple. However, I do not see a "V"-shaped expansion in the residential construction sector. Additionally, Lowe's debt is now elevated, and its potential for near-term buybacks is limited. Lowe's is a relatively expensive stock.

Looking forward, I expect Lowe's to generate 4-5% EPS growth in 2026. I am expecting low single-digit sales growth, reflecting a modest recovery in housing-related spending, a full-year contribution from M&A, partially offset by higher interest expense to fund these deals. That leaves Lowe's with about $12.75-12.90 in 2026 EPS. Shares are trading at over ~21.5x forward earnings. This is expensive for a cyclical retailer with an overleveraged balance sheet and no buybacks likely for 15+ months.

Having recovered so sharply over the past couple of months, I believe Lowe's is now pricing in a sharper rebound in housing-related spending than we are likely to see, which skews risk to the downside. This leaves shares vulnerable to any disappointment, and I peg fair value at closer to $230-235 at an 18x multiple. With over 15% downside, I suggest using the recent recovery in shares to exit Lowe's at an attractive price and reiterate my "Sell" recommendation.

data.title: Envista: Anticipating A FY25 Beat (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I'm upgrading my Envista Holdings Corporation (NVST) rating from "Hold" to "Buy."

NVST is revealing its 4Q/FY25 results next week. My view is that the analysts' forecasts are too conservative. This sets the stage for an earnings beat to re-rate its shares.

The prior Oct. 17, 2025, write-up detailed NVST's divergent prospects, citing competitor remarks and questionnaire results.

Bullish Takeaways From Investor Events

The firm will release full-year financials on Feb. 5.

My take is that its performance would have surpassed expectations.

At the "44th Annual J.P. Morgan (JPM) Healthcare Conference" on Jan. 12 this year, NVST's CEO stressed that "I'd be a touch more optimistic than we were on our Q3 call."

Consensus's FY25 bottom-line projection of $1.14/share is within management's $1.10-$1.15 guide. A positive surprise is highly probable, given the above-mentioned remarks.

NVST shared at JPM's forum that "G&A (General & Administrative" optimization will "continue to be a tailwind for us as we go through the end of Q4." Its 3Q2025 G&A expenses declined by a low-teens percentage on YoY terms. I think it will maintain a lean cost structure in the future.

Also, NVST's JPM meeting commentary indicated that "we're starting to see traction from the customer education" investments for "our implants business." It also disclosed the target of being "quicker than our peers", after merely "growing at the market" in 3Q25.

I believe the group's implants-related operations should expand more strongly for the future. You can imagine that marketing becomes easier when the clients have a better understanding of your products.

Its other divisions are doing well too.

Last month, NVST participated in the "Evercore 8th Annual Healthcare Conference." It highlighted that the Diagnostic segment's "first branded DEXIS (Imprevo intraoral) scanner" witnessed "really good momentum here in fourth quarter."

At Evercore's December 2025 event, the enterprise also spoke about its "good confidence in a sort of a growth trend going forward" for "consumables." That's because this segment features businesses with "good pricing power" which are "growing well above market."

A reduced share count will also boost its EPS.

NVST committed that "we'll continue on that (share purchase) trend as we go forward" as per its Evercore conference disclosures. As of end-3Q25, it had $0.11 billion remaining from its buyback plan that expires at the end of 2026.

Sector-wide Polls Provided Encouraging Insights

"American Dental Association/ADA", Morgan Stanley (MS), and UBS Group (UBS) recently conducted primary research pertaining to the U.S. dental market.

MS issued a report (not publicly available) titled "Survey Says: Baby Steps Forward" on Jan. 14, 2026. It mentioned that the YoY contraction in "practice traffic" improved from 3Q25's -1.6% to 4Q25's -0.9%.

MS had done checks with "75 dental practitioners dispersed across 25 states." Its findings suggested that 13% of respondents used NVST's Spark-branded "clear aligners" in the final quarter of last year. The same metric was a lower 9% for Q3. The company is gaining ground on rivals in the orthodontics space.

Separately, UBS collected "survey responses from 30 orthodontists and 30 general dentists" in December last year. These were published as part of its Jan. 19 note (inaccessible in public) "U.S. Orthodontia Survey."

UBS's work points to "traditional wire and brackets" increasingly being substituted by "clear aligners" in America. The former suffered from a 2.5% YoY decrease in 4Q2025. During the same period, the latter's case numbers grew +4.7%. This bodes well for the Spark solutions sold by NVST.

Also, one-quarter of "General Practitioners/GPs" interviewed by the European bank were keen on buying the firm's DEXIS-branded intra-oral scanner in the near term.

I drew attention to ADA's 3Q25 "poll of dentists" in my previous article. The fourth quarter version was released last week. The proportion of dentists who "made major equipment purchases" rose by +360bps (basis points) sequentially to 4Q25's "25.6%."

The numbers referenced above are consistent with the management's opinion of the rate downcycle. NVST noted at the JPM conference that "as rates continue to come down, it's supportive" for "the equipment category."

I have identified various indicators pointing to a better-than-anticipated 4th quarter showing here.

My prediction is that Envista will register FY2025 earnings of $1.19/share, implying a 4% outperformance versus consensus.

You Should Contemplate These Variant Views

NVST's actual cost-cutting and repurchases might not be as substantial as the market expects.

Macroeconomic weaknesses could translate into lower demand for some of the group's discretionary offerings like implants and aligners.

My Closing Thoughts

I've become a bull on this name.

The market currently values NVST at 19 times the consensus for the next 12 months' P/E. Straumann's (SAUHF) forward earnings multiple is 29x.

The stock's capital appreciation potential is +53% (29/19) benchmarked with its peer. A '25 EPS beat is very likely to act as a catalyst.

data.title: DUST: Fading The Miners At $5,000 Gold
data.description:
The rallies in both Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and Silver (XAGUSD:CUR) over the last 12 months have been nothing short of staggering. With the latter officially hitting $100 per ounce and gold coming within a hair of $5,000, the yellow metal is up nearly 80%, and silver has more than tripled since last January:

Gold miners have held their own as well. Up over 180% in the last year, the mining sector absolutely crushed the broader equity market in 2025 and is off to a stellar start again in 2026. Given the unrelenting rallies in each of these assets to begin the year, is it time to consider allocating some downside protection in the miners? And a better question is, if it is indeed time, is the Direxion Daily Gold Miners Bear 2X Shares (DUST) the right product for hedging a long position in gold miners?

DUST Fund Details and 2x Leverage Risks

  • AUM: $128 million
  • Expense Ratio: 0.93%
  • Dividend: 19.15%
  • Inception: December 2010

While I've covered leveraged funds several times for Seeking Alpha in the past, DUST is not one that I have ever written about before. The objective of the fund is very simple; a bet on a DUST is a leveraged bet against the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX). There are numerous risks to consider before taking a position in any 2x leveraged ETF like DUST. Direxion makes it very clear through its own materials that DUST is not an investment product; it's a trading product:

Trading is different than investing. Use our insights and educational resources to make sure you’re ready to trade in Direxion’s Leveraged and Inverse ETFs.

Since these are actively managed funds that are rebalanced on a daily basis, there is a long-term fund decay that disincentivizes holding for longer than a few days or weeks (at most). You can see the 1-year performance of the fund in the chart below:

At negative 91% in both price and total return, anyone who has actually held DUST for this entire period of time has lost almost all of their capital. Some of this is due to the rebalancing. Most of it is because miners have been in a relentless bull market for the last year. Which brings us to the next key risk: leveraged funds are risky already; when you entertain an inverse leveraged fund, you're not only taking on decay risk, but you're essentially leveraging a short position. There is a time and a place to entertain shorts; a stampeding bull market like the one gold miners are currently enjoying is a very, very difficult short. Hence, DUST has numerous risks. It is for active traders, not investors. It has product risk and thesis risk.

With all that said, is it time to consider a position in DUST? It depends entirely on each individual trader's own personal risk profile and how active that trader is with his/her portfolio on a day-to-day basis. In the next section, I'll explain why I took a very small position in DUST on January 23, 2026, to hedge my mining longs.

Miner Charts

When I make swing trades, I like to look at the assets that I'm entertaining on multiple time frames. Keep in mind, the fundamental setup for gold miners is actually magnificent; fuel prices are low, and gold prices are high. We are looking at GDX (and by extension DUST) through a technical vantage point only. The GDX daily chart shows a fund that is quickly getting as extended as it was in October when it last had a meaningful pullback.

First, GDX is already at an RSI-14 reading of 79. While it could certainly keep going from here, something else that I think warrants a short-term pullback in the miners is how elevated GDX is from its 200-day moving average. Currently, GDX is over 61% above the 200 DMA. The last time GDX was this extended from the 200 was October, and it preceded a 20% correction into early November. On the weekly chart, we again see high RSI readings:

The RSI-14 on the weekly chart is currently just under 79 as well. This is actually lower than the RSI-14 from the October high, which creates a bearish divergence in the indicator. We also see a divergence in volume where the October high was accompanied by a nearly 200 million share volume week. We're not even sniffing that level of volume today. Furthermore, GDX hasn't touched the middle of the Bollinger Band in almost a year. On the monthly chart, we can really get a sense for how intense this rally has been:

Clearly the trendline breakout in 2024 has been a slam-dunk buy for GDX. But at a monthly RSI-14 of 87, GDX has literally never been this overbought in its 20-year history. Given the level of extremes that we're seeing on essentially every time frame for GDX, I think taking a small shot on DUST is a perfectly fine way to express a short-term bearish gold miners thesis. Again, one must be mindful of their personal risk tolerances, as these are not investment funds. But given gold and silver have both essentially reached the big round psychological numbers of $100 and $5,000 per ounce, I wouldn't rule out profit taking as January approaches a close. In that event, it's doubtful miners would continue higher without the metals participating.

Closing Thoughts

A question that readers might have is, 'Why not just sell the mining stocks if you think they've topped out?' This is a good question and, admittedly, a much safer strategy than trading DUST. The reason why I like using 2x leveraged funds opportunistically is because I don't play the options game, and I see leveraged funds as a viable alternative to options that could theoretically expire worthless. Second, I don't think the bull market in miners is over and, thus, don't want to sell them. Timing is difficult. I would personally rather take a small shot on DUST and take the trade-off quickly in the event that I'm wrong than sell out of my mining stocks.

Of course, this is a matter of personal preference. But for those who are able to abide by trading rules and stick to them without emotions, I think 2x leveraged funds can be very useful trading tools. While I have core investments, I'm a trader at heart. I have a very small position in DUST at a $4.74 cost basis. I expect to take the trade-off when GDX closes the January 9th gap at $93 per share. That would be a 13% correction in GDX that would figure to take shares of DUST close to $6. If GDX moves above $112, I'll close out the trade and take the small loss. I'm certainly not going to call DUST a 'buy' given its uselessness as an investment. But I don't think it's a 'sell' on January 23rd, 2026 either.

data.title: Cohen & Steers: Cheap Enough To Buy As Income Comes Back Into Focus (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Cohen & Steers (CNS) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about a quarter of their value. While the company is controlling what it can fairly well, appetite for real estate-oriented investments has been very poor, which has limited fund flows and compressed its multiple. I last covered shares in July, rating CNS a "Sell," and since then, the stock has lost 12% while the market has gained 8%, meaningful relative underperformance that justifies the rating. With shares around my $65 price target and some hopes real estate activity could be bottoming, now is a good time to revisit shares.

In the company's fourth quarter, Cohen & Steers earned $0.81 per share, which matched estimates as revenue grew nearly 3% to $144 million. For the full year, CNS earned $3.09; I was expecting $2.95-$3.20, so results were right in line with my expectations. Operating income was also up 3% to $52 million as its operating margin expanded 30 bps from last year to 36.4%. Management targets compensation expenses being about 40% of revenue, and in Q4, it came in at 39%. Compensation is over 60% of the company's expense, as is the case for most investment managers. Compensation was down $200k from last year to $56.2 million as CNS managed headcount and variable compensation given the muted growth environment for its products.

Weak REIT Demand Has Limited Growth

AUM ended the quarter at $90.5 billion, which fell a bit sequentially, given year-end dynamics where many funds pay a larger dividend. As you can see, average AUM was up slightly. Relative to last year, CNS ended the year with ~5% more AUM, reflecting ~2% of inflows and ~3% of appreciation. This modest AUM growth during a pronounced equity market rally speaks to the fact that investor appetite has been geared towards higher growth sectors like tech than income-oriented ones like real estate.

Importantly, 95% of its funds are beating benchmarks on a 1-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year basis. This means that when investor demand towards REITs and U.S. real estate improves, CNS should be well-positioned to capture those flows. CNS cannot control how well real estate does; it can only control its own performance. Cohen & Steers is a perfect example of a company controlling what it can well but being stuck in a sector that is struggling, which is preferable to the reverse situation in my view. Investment preferences tend to be cyclical, and at some point, real estate will be a more favored sector; the question is when that turn occurs.

Generally speaking, REITs are popular among income-oriented investors, given their higher dividends and tax advantages. Higher interest rates have increased competition for these investors, as there have been 5+% yield opportunities in fixed income as the Fed raised rates. Additionally, real estate valuations tend to be inversely correlated with interest rates, all else equal. As such, the higher rate environment over the past four years has challenged both investor flows and valuations of its existing AUM. As you can see below, the REIT index (REIT:IND) is down over the past four years, significantly underperforming the S&P. This is just a significant headwind to CNS's REIT-oriented investment platform. To the extent rates have likely peaked, these pressures should be diminishing going forward.

Momentum May Be Turning

Flows were $1.28 billion for the quarter and were aided by a $513 million rights offering on one of its closed-end funds. Inflows have now been positive five of the last six quarters. This was the strongest net flow environment in over a year. Excluding the rights offering (which is more akin to a one-time inflow and cannot be frequently repeated), flows were comparable to Q4 of last year. Additionally, its open-end funds had no net inflows, and instead, it registered some institutional wins, speaking to ongoing weakness in demand from retail investors. While flows were positive this quarter, the composition of flows was a bit weaker than I like to see. I expect a seasonal slowdown of flows in H1 2026. That said, I do think the investment environment for the real estate sector should be better this year, given improved capital market access for CRE transactions and less competition from fixed income.

As you can see below, the company gets the narrow majority of its revenue from its open-end fund fees. Aside from that, it also offers separately managed accounts for large institutional investors that provide more customized investment solutions. Cohen & Steers also manages a suite of closed-end funds, which provide a stable, recurring revenue base but over the long term offer less growth potential than open-ended funds, which can have consistent inflows (the only way for closed-end funds to generate inflows is to sell additional shares via a secondary or a rights offering).

CNS is working to diversify its fund offerings and revenue sources. Its nontraded REIT has returned 10.4% annualized since its inception two years ago. CNS has historically focused on publicly traded products, but private funds have been an increasing source of investor focus. CNS is trying to catch up to other alternative asset managers in the space, and it is starting to see some incremental investor demand.

It is also focusing on active ETFs, and there were $175 million of inflows, including $25 million of its own seed capital. The company now has 5 active ETFs with external assets of $290 million, just about 0.3% of its total asset base. Over the past 30 years, we have seen ETFs boom and take market share away from mutual funds. The initial stage was passive ETFs, directly tracking an index, which gained popularity given low fees and tradability. Now, we are seeing managers increasingly launch actively managed products in ETF format, given easier recordkeeping demands and tradability. CNS's business here is still in its infancy, but these offerings should ensure it has the right product suite to meet all client demand. Over time, I would expect its share of AUM in ETFs to rise at the expense of mutual funds. To a large extent, I view these products as protecting its existing revenue base rather than being a driver of substantial incremental revenue.

With an Excellent Balance Sheet and Fair Valuation, Shares Are Now a 'Buy'

Cohen & Steers has an excellent balance sheet with $403 million of cash and investments. Against this, it carries no debt. This amounts to nearly ~$8/share. Because of its excellent financial position, I view its dividend as highly secure. Shares offer a 3.6% dividend yield. CNS does not materially repurchase stock, instead doing periodic special dividends, its last being in 2021. CNS maintains strong liquidity to support seed capital for new funds and strategies, though with this balance likely to approach $500 million by year-end, a special payout over the next year is a definite possibility in my view.

Looking forward, I expect CNS to see a somewhat better environment for its products in 2026 than in 2025, especially as its push into active ETFs should support some incremental flows. I am targeting ~5% AUM growth as a result, which should enable 0-30 bps of further operating margin expansion as it continues to control compensation costs. This points to $3.25-$3.40 in EPS this year. Netting out its cash position, shares are now trading at 17.5x earnings. After their underperformance since I last covered CNS, its multiple is now much more attractive, considering its excellent balance sheet, strong fund performance, and improving macro backdrop.

To be clear, I am not expecting a sharp acceleration in demand for REIT-oriented funds, but I expect some improvement. If rates fall more sharply (I expect 2 Fed rate cuts), that would be a further tailwind. Still, given the recurring fee-based nature of its business, I view 18-20x earnings as reasonable. This suggests a fair value in the $70-$73 range, including its net cash and investments position. As such, a "Sell" is no longer merited, and the question is whether CNS should be a "Buy" or a "Hold." I expect shares to generate a ~9%-12% return, which puts them on the cusp of my 10% 'Buy' rating threshold. While a close call, I am moving shares to "Buy" as I see the environment in 2026 as being favorable, and a possible special dividend late this year could add to upside. While I do not see CNS returning to its $100 high anytime soon, 2026 should be a year of recovery that now makes the stock attractive.

This article updates my review of October 2023 in light of recent performance and current holdings.

IGRO strategy

iShares International Dividend Growth ETF (IGRO) was launched on 5/17/2016 and tracks the Morningstar Global ex-U.S. Dividend Growth Index. IGRO has a portfolio of 557 stocks, a 12-month distribution yield of 2.51%, and a total expense ratio of 0.15%. Distributions are paid quarterly. As described in the prospectus by iShares, an eligible company must be part of the Morningstar® Global Markets ex-U.S. Index and:

  • pay a qualified dividend and not be classified as a REIT,
  • have at least five years of uninterrupted annual dividend growth,
  • have an earnings payout ratio of less than 75%,
  • not be in the top decile of the Morningstar Global Markets ex-U.S. Index in its region ranked on dividend yield.

The portfolio turnover rate was 34% in the most recent fiscal year. This article will use as a benchmark an ex-U.S. equity index represented by the iShares Core MSCI Total International Stock ETF (IXUS).

Portfolio

The fund invests mostly in large and mega cap companies (about 89% of asset value). Europe represents about 48% of assets, and the second region is Asia with 36%. Japan is the top country (20.3%), followed by Canada (13.5%), Switzerland (13%), and France (10.2%). Other countries are below 8%. China, Taiwan, and Hong Kong weigh 7.8% in aggregate, so direct exposure to geopolitical risks related to China is moderate. data.title: Meritage Homes Remains A Top Tier Play
data.description:
In my view, the housing market is one of the most interesting spaces to play in when it comes to the stock market right now. This is because there are two opposing forces, and one of them must eventually win. On the one hand, you have years of inflationary pressures and high interest rates aimed at combatting them that have resulted in declining home sales and, over the last couple of years now, a drop in home prices. But on the other hand, you have a perpetual large shortage of homes, with the number of units short amounting to 4.7 million. 8.1 million families are living with somebody who is not related to them. My own view is that the oncoming recession will cause a rapid decline in interest rates. We might see some deflation associated with the materials that go into home building. And these two activities will go a long way toward making houses more affordable, leading to a boom in production.

One of the best players in this market to take into consideration as we lead up to that point is Meritage Homes (MTH). In my last article about the company, published in early October of 2025, I acknowledged industry weakness. But at the end of the day, shares were cheap enough and my belief in how this industry will change moving forward with strong enough to justify maintaining my ‘strong buy’ rating on the stock. Since then, shares have pulled back by 1.6%. That is admittedly worse than the 2.1% rise that the S&P 500 saw. But honestly, I still firmly believe in my previous assessment.

Naturally, I will change my mind as new data comes in. And that new data is just around the corner. After the market closes on January 28th, the management team at Meritage Homes will be announcing financial results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Unfortunately, we should expect more of the same. But so long as something major and unexpected is not to happen that negatively affects the business, I am unlikely to change my assessment when those results come out.

A solid play in this environment

The only new data that investors currently have when it comes to Meritage Homes covers through the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue was $1.42 billion. Not surprisingly, this was below the $1.60 billion the company reported a year earlier. This decline was driven by a drop in the number of homes closed. This number contracted from 3,942 units in 2024 to 3,685 units in 2025. But that's not all. The average price of a home closed also dropped, contracting from $402,000 to $380,000. This perfectly reflects my introduction to this article where I talked about broader market conditions. And it is part of a trend that has been happening for a couple of years now.

Unfortunately, declining revenue is not the only problem that companies have in this market. They often have relatively high fixed costs that they experience deleveraging of sales from, and many home builders are having to contend with the unenviable position of offering higher incentives in order to get buyers to bite. This helps to explain why, even though the decline in revenue was only 10.8%, net income dropped a whopping 49.3%, falling from $196 million down to $99.3 million. Other profitability metrics contracted also. Although the worsening for operating cash flow was modest, with it going from negative $92 million to negative $96.5 million, the drop when it comes to adjusted operating cash flow was sharp. It plummeted from $221.9 million to $131.1 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company dropped from $245.8 million to $126.1 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Once again, revenue and profits declined. Cash flows largely worsened also. This is part of a trend for the company driven by weak industry conditions. And even though some of the data has held up better than what I have seen from other players in the space, the company still reported declining homes closed and closing prices.

These aren't the only data points that are problematic. Backlog, for instance, has taken quite a hit. At the end of the third quarter of 2025, Meritage Homes had backlog of 1,699 homes. That was down from the 2,284 homes in backlog reported for the third quarter of 2024. What's more, the average price of a home in backlog dropped from $408,000 to $394,000.

The good news is that there were a couple of bright spots. Even though the cancellation rate for the institution worsened from 10% in the third quarter of 2024 to 11% in the third quarter of 2025, that is far better than it could have been. I have seen some home builders, over the last few years, periodically have cancellation rates in excess of 20%. So to see only a small increase during such a difficult time for the industry is a testament to the quality of the business.

We also can't forget net new orders. And this is where a real bright spot for the company is. The firm went from having 3,512 new homes ordered in the third quarter of 2024 to having 3,636 homes ordered the same time of 2025. A similar year over year increase occurred when it came to the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time a year earlier. But even these data points have a bit of bad to them. I say this because, as the chart below illustrates, the average price of a home ordered dropped, falling from $406,000 in the third quarter of 2024 to $389,000 in 2025. A similar drop can be seen when looking at the nine months of each fiscal year provided.

If we project out results based on what we have seen already for 2025, then we would get net profits for the year of $483.3 million. Adjusted operating cash flow would be about $549.3 million, while EBITDA would come in at $602.8 million. Using these estimates, and also using the results that the company achieved for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart below how the company is currently priced.

In the chart below, meanwhile, you can see how the firm is priced compared to five other similar firms. The pricing is not fantastic on a relative basis, even if it is very appealing on an absolute basis. On a price to earnings basis, for instance, four of the five companies ended up being cheaper than our target is. And three ended up being cheaper when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach. Only on a price to earnings basis was Meritage Homes incredibly compelling, coming in cheaper than any of the five banks that I stacked it up against.

When relative valuation looks like this, a very bullish outlook is typically not the route that I go. But there is another reason I am bullish about the company. This is the fact that the $1.10 billion in net debt on its books means that its overall leverage is very low. In the chart below, you can see how the net leverage ratio of the business of 1.82 stacks up against other comparable firms. Only one of these is better off than it is on this basis. The others have more leverage, with some of them having substantially more.

In addition to having this strong balance sheet, the company has another thing going forward. And this is a fact that a large and growing share of the lots that it has are already owned. Typically, when times get tough, you would prefer that a larger share of lots are optioned. But when you have low leverage and a large portfolio of lots, it means that the company is in a good position from a long term standpoint. It means it can weather the storm that it's in, and that when property prices start to rise again, it will be well positioned. It also means the company has significant financial flexibility with which to grow while property prices are on the cheap. As the image below illustrates, it controls 80,836 lots. That's up from the 74,819 reported the previous year. This works out to about 5.3 years of supply, and 69% of those lots are owned compared to the 64% that were owned a year prior.

At the start of this article, I mentioned that management will be announcing financial results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year after the market closes on January 28th. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue will come in at around $1.51 billion. This would represent a drop from the $1.62 billion the company reported a year earlier. And with that decline in sales would also come a drop in profits, with earnings per share expected to plummet from $4.72 to $1.57. This would mean a decline in net income from $172.6 million to only $111.8 million. In the table below, you can see certain cash flow metrics for the final quarter of 2024. In all likelihood, these metrics will worsen also.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I would say that while market conditions might weaken, and fundamental performance might lag, Meritage Homes is well positioned. The company is cheap on an absolute basis and its net debt situation is positive. While it is possible that upcoming earnings figures might change my mind, it would take a lot for that to happen. At the end of the day, I believe that maintaining a ‘strong buy’ rating is the appropriate move.

data.title: Carlisle Companies: Residential Construction Driven Momentum
data.description:
Introduction and Investment Thesis

I am bullish on Carlisle Companies Incorporated (NYSE: CSL), driven by a projected low federal interest rate ranging between 3.5% to 3.75% from 3.8% at the end of 2025. This is set to benefit home builders by reducing the cost of construction loans used to finance new residential houses. Recent research done on 1,000 participants indicates that over 88% of participants are looking forward to purchasing a home in 2026. Only 78% are a bit indifferent due to affordability, but 58% state that lowered mortgage rates would expedite their plans to own a residential house.

In my opinion, when builders experience market tailwinds such as reduced interest rates and lowered mortgage rates, it becomes affordable to build more residential houses. Now that the mortgage rate is expected to drop from 6.7% in 2025 to 6.0% in 2026, I believe this is likely to improve the demand for residential houses, which is a gain for manufacturers of construction materials. Let me give you a different picture. About 44% of millennials in the U.S. are willing to own a home by committing half of their income to housing. This includes about 49% willing to consider even when the interest rate is above 6%. This indicates that if the interest rate, which is around 3.75% drops to the projected 3.5% as previously stated, there will be an increase in residential house construction at affordable rates. Adding mortgage reduction, I believe that 44% of 74 million millennials, who are 32 million of them, have a chance to own homes in 2026.

Looking back after this promising market tailwind, Carlisle’s revenues are majorly generated from construction materials at 74% and only 26% are generated from weatherproofing technologies. This is a clear indication that the company is best positioned for this residential housing demand. From what I see, I believe this is the optimism that has resulted in Carlisle’s YTD price return at 12.11% beating the S&P 500 at 0.44% since January 2026.

Let me now dive deeper into why I think residential constructions are a major driver of Carlisle’s revenue and why I believe it warrants a bullish rating.

Company Brief

Carlisle Companies Incorporated manufactures building envelope products globally. This company operates through Carlisle Construction Materials and Carlisle Weatherproofing Technologies. It offers roofing systems, insulation, waterproofing, and air vapour barriers, HVAC solutions, and coatings under brands such as panels, Hunter Panels, and so forth. The company was incorporated in 1917, and it is headquartered in Scottsdale, Arizona.

Residential Construction Rebound In 2026

Before I get to the depth of residential construction rebound, I will begin by giving a broader picture. From a broader perspective, the U.S. residential construction market is growing steadily at a CAGR of 4.53% between 2026 and 2031. The current U.S. residential construction market volume is valued at $1.41 trillion, and it is estimated to reach $1.76 trillion by 2031.

By type of construction, 69.05% of overall U.S residential construction is new-build activity. It demonstrates that the demand for new residential houses is there. This is mainly driven by the falling mortgage rates and about 13 million millennial households who are actively in need of residential houses. Right now, the rising housing costs are a major financing challenge delaying the housing sector, but the supply challenge is also to a larger extent, an issue. The U.S. government is beginning with addressing the supply challenge by lowering interest rates and mortgage rates. This supply issue has been discussed so far by the Trump Administration which has resulted to a recent directive of $200 billion mortgage-backed securities purchase with intent of lowering 2026 mortgage rates. Adding that the interest rate is projected to reach 3.5% this year, this might spur the financial aspect of residential housing construction. As a result, the construction financing loans are likely to go down in 2026. This forms the basis of why I believe there are high chances of high demand for construction materials from both new constructions and renovations.

In 2025, the construction investments for both residential and nonresidential constructions were sluggish due to high material costs and uncertainty in policies. Residential constructions have been significantly affected by this lagging and relatively volatile spending, but I believe that this is likely to ease in 2026 following the lowered interest rate and mortgage reductions which are likey to drive an upward spiral. For instance, the nationwide existing home sales are estimated to hit 14% in 2026, up from 0% in 2025, and new home sales are expected to show a 5% gain, up from -2%.

From an inward perspective, Carlisle is positioned to benefit from this potential residential construction growth. I earlier stated that so far, the majority of revenues, at 74% are generated from construction materials and the remaining weatherproofing technologies at 26%. Given that 67% of these revenues are from construction materials for remodeling and replacement, and 33% on new construction, I think Carlisle is best positioned for a potential broadening new construction materials demand likely to soar in 2026.

Residential revenue represents around 8% which can be linked to persistent high housing costs that scaled down the demand for housing. With the projected financial solution (Interest rate reduction) and supply solution (Mortgage rate reduction), I believe the company has what it takes to gain from this projected demand in the residential building materials segment.

So far, Carlisle Companies has been awarded by Extel as the most honored company for the third consecutive year. This indicates Carlisle’s strong performance in corporate leadership in the construction business. In 2025, the company was ranked as the first out of eight categories in the Homebuilder & Products sector, which shows its consistent excellence and credibility in the construction materials sector.

Financials and Valuation

Carlisle’s top-line trend has exhibited volatility over the last couple of years, which demonstrates the uncertainties around housing. For example, there is a significant increase in revenues of $4.45 billion, up from -3.36% YoY growth in 2021 to 42.03% growth YoY in 2022. Notably, a significant fluctuation of revenues followed in 2023 with revenue dipping by about 15.8% YoY, before a 9% YoY growth rebound in 2024.

This signals the cyclical and volatility nature of the housing industry driven by several factors such as unstable interest rates over the last couple of years.

Currently, the revenue is trailing at $5.015 billion, and according to consensus estimates, the revenue for FY2025 is likely to plateau at $5.00 billion. Both federal interest rates and mortgage rates have been above 3.75% and 6.7% respectively, and have slowed home building due to the high costs of borrowing. Now, with an initiative such as $200 billion in the purchase of mortgage-based bonds, this is a blueprint of residential housing affordability, especially to around 32 million millennials in America actively looking for a residential house.

On profitability, the same fluctuation in revenue is also seemingly having an equal impact on the bottom line. Since 2022, as I noted above on the top-line, the bottom-line has been fluctuating, indicating the housing sector’s volatility. But the net income trend shows that Carlisle has a solid execution capability, looking at 2022 revenues, which were the highest at $4.45 billion and a net income of $924 million. Compared to FY2024, when the revenue hit $5.003 billion, the net income was a 5-year high at $1.311 billion, indicating strong execution. Notably, for more than 5 years, the company has consistently maintained profitability despite the macroeconomic uncertainties which further supports its strong execution.

I believe that moving forward, the current adjusted federal interest rates and reduced mortgage rates previously stated will be a major driver of this company’s earnings rebound. This is because the net income still lags that of 2024, trailing at $776 million, down from $1.312 billion in 2024. The historical trend in the last 5 years shows that Carlisle’s EPS has been beating analysts’ estimates. With FY2026 backing of strong fundamentals, I believe there is a grounded reason to believe in the possibility of EPS beating 2026 estimates. This projected growth in 2026 is likely to drive an EPS of about $25.

I will now value CSL using the P/S multiple to that of its peers, Armstrong World Industries, Inc. (NYSE: AWI), and Owens Corning (NYSE: OC). CSL P/S ratio is at 3.12x, which is below AWL’s P/S ratio of 5.15x, and a distance higher than OC, which is at 0.91x.

With the market tailwinds and market leadership CSL has demonstrated over the years and possible dominance in the supply of residential construction materials, I think the market undervalues CSL. Well, AWI has a higher revenue growth rate YoY for the FY2026 at 6.91% resulting in $1.75 billion, compared to CSL, whose revenue growth rate YoY for FY2026 is 2.37% resulting in $5.12 billion. Clearly, CSL is demonstrating market dominance, which is why I think this is an undervaluation. OC has a revenue growth rate for the FY2026 of -3.35% representing $9.79 billion, which also indicates that CSL is dominating the construction material market. As such, I think CSL has a high upside potential given its best market positioning, which is why I am optimistic. In my view, a revaluation to about 5x is plausible.

Investment Risk

According to the investor presentation, only 33% of Carlisle Construction Materials’ revenue and 56% of Carlisle Weatherproofing Technologies’ revenue come from new construction, with the majority tied to remodeling and replacement. So far, new constructions are facing delays, affordability challenges, and cancellations due to high interest rates and weakened consumer spending. If the interest rates and mortgage rates do not reduce as projected, these conditions may persist or worsen in 2026. This could affect Carlisle’s $5.0 billion revenue base and its Vision 2030 target of around 5% organic growth despite recording a solid re-roofing demand.

Conclusion

I have a bullish stance on this company, and I hinge on possible interest rate reduction and looming mortgage reduction following $200 billion purchase of mortgage bonds. This is a hint of a potentially strong 2026 residential housing demand, which will spur the demand for construction materials.

data.title: We Could Be In For A Flat Market In 2026
data.description:
Flat 2026? October Was The Nasdaq's last high. What to do?

Where are we headed? The Nasdaq is flat, and the S&P 500 is nearly so.

In this article, I want to explore how important the biggest names on the Nasdaq and the S&P 500 are to the state of US-based equities. Without the 10 “Trillionaires” on an upswing, who will propel the market to new highs? I am not saying a down or flat market is a certainty, as always, I am talking about risks. No one is predicting a bear market for 2026, and I am not making that case here. I am just putting the notion that the indexes could be flat for 2026 on the table and evaluating the risks. You are going to hear me say risk several times in this article, as someone who actively manages my stock portfolio, risk is extremely important. Most of my loyal readers at this point have heard me mention this before. Since it’s a new year, I hope you don’t mind if I talk about how I approach risk again. I have talked about how I use cash as part of an active hedging strategy and also how I use options and inverse ETFs for hedging. Using options is a very powerful tool, and before you toy with them, go to your brokerage firm’s education section and learn as much as you can about them before you use them. Now with that PSA out of the way, let’s summarize: I am not predicting a bear market, I am not even stating that a flat market is a certainty. I am writing about this topic because I am thinking through what might be ahead of us. That, despite the optimism in the financial media, I want to build my own picture of where the market is going. Again, if you have been reading my content for a while, I am always saying “this, or that will happen.” Then, as the week or month transpires, I compare what is happening to what I thought would happen and adjust accordingly.

I have been sharing a much more positive view with my investment group. For instance, I was taking for granted that 2025 would see 7000 to 7200 on the S&P 500. I was expecting a strong year-end rally, but the Santa Claus Rally was barely there, especially for the Nasdaq. The large year-end rally started early in October, reaching 6986, and then fizzled out. I think it is right and proper for a stock optimist like me to examine whether the first half (or even all) of 2026 might be treading water for the larger indexes. The state of being bullish is not the same as belonging to a team or a religion, so thinking about the underperformance of the stock indexes is not disloyalty. The only thing I am loyal to is growing the value of my portfolio. I will probably say this more than twice. I am talking about risks, not certitude.

It is fantastic that the rest of the market is outperforming, but take out these 10, then the market has to pull that 40% to 60% weight along with it. The alternative to up is not only down but staying in place. As a close observer of the stock market, I have to weigh the risks to the overall market. As always, the general trend of the market influences nearly all stock valuation is up. This is not merely due to the “Animal Spirits” of bullishness but because of the dominance of ETFs. On the other hand, a crash of a few giant stocks mechanically reverberates throughout ETF members and depresses all. Likewise, the upthrust of several megacaps will boost all stocks in that sector because of sector ETFs and the larger indexes. In fact, that is what the Mag-7 and their ilk have been doing for quite a few years. Further stating the obvious, is that they deserved the higher valuation because they have been growing revenue and cash flow from existing businesses and creating new businesses. The Magificent-7 are (were?) truly magnificent. Everyone knows that, and most of us at this point own most of them, if not all. So we are all subject to their performance. Going into the height of earnings season, we are hoping that most participants have been trimming their tech titan holdings, so that if the bigs do outperform, the stocks will start moving up again. The more I think about it, the more this notion makes sense. The big-cap names are fully valued; they will only go higher if they really surprise to the upside. Perhaps their performance could nudge them a bit closer to their old highs, but that isn’t enough.

The most popular stocks tend to be the growth stocks.

In the indexes, the top stocks are weighted over 40% for the S&P 500 (cite) and about 60.41 % for the Nasdaq composite -100(cite). Much is being made of how well the Small caps and the 490 stocks of the S&P 500 perform. I ask you to look at this ratio, and ask yourself, is this really the true picture of the stock market? We take for granted that higher weighting goes to the bigger names, but if everyone has piled into these names, pushing up the P/Es, maybe the tech titans need to grow into those valuations for a bit. Meaning maybe they create cash flow and profits for Q4 and exceed expectations, and even raise expectations, but remain at their current level. I am not predicting a bear market, just perhaps a flat one.

Looking at the Trillionaire Club's recent performance, you might be a bit unimpressed

The issue is whether the indexes can make new highs without the Trillionaires: Nvidia (NVDA), Alphabet (GOOGL), Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Broadcom (AVGO), Meta Platforms (META), Tesla (TSLA), Taiwan Semi (TSM), Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B). Quibble with TSM and BRK.B. The table below shows the weighting in the S&P500 via the S&P500 ETF (SPY). This makes sense as we are talking about ETFs, and also how most people invest in this index. Yes, I mistakenly had TSM on the S&P 500 list even though it is a Taiwanese company. Still, TSM is a component of the Semiconductor index and ETF (SMH), and semiconductors have been the big performers. So even though it’s not in the SPY, it is still in the “Trillionaire Club.” TSM, as with NVDA and other titans, is certainly an influence in all kinds of tech and growth ETFs. TSM stays in the picture.

Almost all of the names below are more than 10% lower than their highs.

Most investors invest in ETFs, and the biggest ones are cap-weighted

Everyone seems to be celebrating the strength of 490 S&P500 stocks compared to the “Trillionaire Club”; I am too. However, as I’ve said, a rally without their leadership seems to be capping the ability for new highs. That is really the key to this whole article, really. This means that owners of these most popular ETFs have a higher risk of underperforming this year for the average investor.

I would not necessarily urge someone who puts money away every month to change in mid-stream, or if you have a money manager to override their advice and sell stocks. Let me say that dollar cost averaging is a powerful way to build wealth, and stick with your plan. However, if you are like my investment group, we actively manage our portfolios. That means that stock picking matters, and how you select stocks matters. However, there are some safe tactics that, if I were starting fresh or allocating new funds to an investment. I would look at some Equal-weight versions of many of the popular cap-weighted indexes.

Now I am not casting shade at the Cap-Weighted indexes and the ETFs that are based on them. They work brilliantly; of course, you want to give more weight to the best stocks. The S&P 500 rearranges the weighting every quarter, which means the best get a bigger share than the slower names. My reasoning for why the big names haven’t gone anywhere but down for most of the last 3 months is that perhaps they are richly but fully valued. The message of the market is that these stocks are so huge and highly valued that they need to accrue a higher level of earnings to justify a higher price. If you want to say that there is a fear that the AI revolution has pulled forward valuation, I won’t dissuade you. I am a firm believer that Generative AI will bring tremendous value to corporate America. That said, there will be bumps along the way; maybe that is what is holding back the appreciation in the bigs.

So if I were looking for an adjustment in my ETF holdings, to allow for smaller stocks to shine through, this is what I would do.

There are equal weight ETF that mimic the S&P 500, and others

  • Equal-Weight S&P 500 (RCP)

  • Equal-Weight Nasdaq 100 (QQQE)

  • Equal-Weight Biotech (XBI)

  • Equal-Weight Semi Conductors (XSD)

Also Look at mid-sized and small cap stocks indexes

  • Midcap Russell ETF (IWR)

  • S&P 600 ETF (IJR)

I have tried to locate the ETFs with the largest number of shares outstanding. There are tons of ETFs out there. If you think the Tech Trillionaires will keep their rapid growth for a few more years, this change in tactic is not for you. At least this thought exercise might open us up to the notion that we may have to look elsewhere for alpha if the Big Caps are stuck in neutral.

Here is a more positive alternative

No one should be feeling bearish after reading this article. If you are a stock picker and you are good at selecting your own stocks, you have a good opportunity to outperform the indexes. Personally, my investment group and I operate totally without using ETFs. We targeted select sectors that I believe are emerging growth and should positively sustain our portfolios in the future. We have been about 2 years early, but we have individual small-cap biotechs, medtech, Space, Defense, and Aviation. Yes, now names like Rocket Labs (RKLB) and Planet Labs (PL) might be familiar, but we accumulated them when they were brand new and quite cheap. Even with all that, I do own the big-cap tech names, and I even recently bought Microsoft (MSFT) this week when it was just at 488.99, a tad below my 20% discount rule, at -19.8% (but good enough). I don’t own AAPL, not a fan, and I don’t own TSLA or BRK.B. That leaves AMZN, GOOGL, NVDA, and META.

I am, however, underweight compared to these big names weighing in the indexes. The bigcaps are about 20% of my portfolio, and I would add to them if they fell into my -20% discount filter. The discount filter rule is just one of the tactics in our trading and investing approach.

What do I really think?

This exercise helped me confirm the notion that if these big caps don’t start moving, it will hurt everyone but the best individual stock investors. I hedged this past week using the inverse Nasdaq-100 ETF (SQQQ) and also using call options for the inverse S&P 500 (SPXS). I made a little money on them, but I am happy to be a loser, knowing that I have a little insurance against a sharp move to the downside. I also set aside cash the week before, in case some great stock like MSFT falls into my 20% discount rule. Speaking of MSFT, they report this week, so I will be ready in case it or any other great stock gives me that great discount. Let me leave you with one more positive thought: all of these big-cap names that are underwater don’t have to make new highs or even hit the old ones. If they lift 5% or more, we could easily make new highs, and everyone will be happy as long as the 490 does their part.

Have a great week, trading everyone!
